note_data.title: Further equilibria
keyPoints: Transition elements form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals and are found in the d-block of the periodic table
Scandium and zinc are in the d-block but are not transition elements because their ions do not have incomplete d subshells
Group 2 carbonates and nitrates become more thermally stable as you go down the group due to decreasing charge density of cations
Smaller Group 2 ions have greater charge density and greater polarising effect on neighbouring negative ions
Group 2 hydroxide solubility increases down the group while sulfate solubility decreases down the group
For hydroxides, lattice enthalpy falls faster than enthalpy change of hydration, making solution more exothermic down the group
For sulfates, lattice enthalpy falls slower than hydration enthalpy, making solution more endothermic down the group
The 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital, so 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions

definitions.definition: Elements that form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
The concentration of charge in a given volume - smaller ions have greater charge density
Compounds with very low solubilities, like magnesium hydroxide

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the definition or value without explanation
Give the reason using equilibrium principles and Le Chatelier's principle
Show all working with correct units and significant figures
Use given data to work out the answer with reasoning shown

modelAnswer.question: A buffer solution contains 0.10 mol dm⁻³ ethanoic acid and 0.15 mol dm⁻³ sodium ethanoate. Calculate the pH of this buffer. (Ka for ethanoic acid = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³)
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct use of Henderson-Hasselbalch equation

commonMistakes: Thinking scandium and zinc are transition elements when their ions have complete or empty d subshells
Confusing the solubility trends - hydroxides become more soluble down Group 2 while sulfates become less soluble
Forgetting that 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming transition metal ions
Not understanding that thermal stability increases down Group 2 due to decreased polarising power of larger cations
examinerTips: Remember that more exothermic enthalpy of solution means higher solubility
Know that smaller Group 2 ions are more polarising due to greater charge density, leading to lower thermal stability of their compounds
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Buffer action diagram showing weak acid HA and conjugate base A⁻ removing added H⁺ and OH⁻ ions respectively, with equilibrium arrows and the resulting pH stability
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate Kw at different temperatures and explain how pH of pure water changes with temperature
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — students had to calculate the pH of a buffer solution and explain how it resists pH change when acid is added
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying the correct expression for the solubility product of calcium fluoride
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (7 marks) — comprehensive question requiring calculation of Ksp from solubility data and predicting precipitation when solutions are mixed

workedExample.question: The solubility of silver chloride in water at 25°C is 1.3 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³. Calculate the solubility product, Ksp, of silver chloride at this temperature. [3 marks]
steps: Step 1: Write the equilibrium equation: AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq)
Step 2: Write the Ksp expression: Ksp = [Ag⁺][Cl⁻]
Step 3: From stoichiometry, if solubility = 1.3 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³, then [Ag⁺] = [Cl⁻] = 1.3 × 10⁻⁵ mol dm⁻³
Step 4: Substitute into Ksp expression: Ksp = (1.3 × 10⁻⁵) × (1.3 × 10⁻⁵) = 1.69 × 10⁻¹⁰ mol² dm⁻⁶
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct equilibrium equation, 1 mark for correct Ksp expression with proper stoichiometry, 1 mark for correct numerical answer with units

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to include correct units for equilibrium constants' — always check if Ksp needs mol² dm⁻⁶ or mol³ dm⁻⁹ units depending on the compound

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused common ion effect with buffer action' — remember common ion effect reduces solubility by shifting equilibrium, while buffers resist pH change through conjugate acid-base pairs

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often forgot that Kw changes with temperature while pH of pure water is not always 7' — at higher temperatures, Kw increases so pure water has pH < 7 but is still neutral
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a solubility curve showing how ionic compound solubility varies with temperature. The diagram should have: x-axis labeled 'Temperature (°C)', y-axis labeled 'Solubility (g per 100g water)', curved line showing increasing solubility with temperature for most salts (like KNO₃), annotation showing that some compounds like Ca(OH)₂ have decreasing solubility curves, and clear indication of saturation point at any given temperature.
note_data.quickRecap: Transition elements have incomplete d orbitals in their stable ions, excluding scandium and zinc. Group 2 shows clear trends: thermal stability of carbonates/nitrates increases down the group due to decreasing cation polarising power. Solubility trends are opposite for hydroxides (increase) and sulfates (decrease) down Group 2, explained by the relative rates of change in lattice enthalpy and hydration enthalpy.
